title: Congee with Century Egg

seo.imageAlt: A bowl of creamy Congee with Century Egg, garnished with green onions and ginger, ready to be served for breakfast.
seo.imageCaption: Enjoy a warm and comforting bowl of homemade Congee with Century Egg, a classic Chinese breakfast dish.

description: Congee with Century Egg, also known as Pidan Congee, is a comforting and savory Chinese breakfast staple. This creamy rice porridge features the unique, umami-rich flavor of century eggs combined with fragrant ginger and tender rice. It is a hearty and deeply satisfying dish, perfect for a nourishing start to your day or a soothing meal when you need comfort.

ingredients.notes: peeled and thinly sliced or julienned

instructions.title: Prepare the Rice
instructions.text: Rinse the short-grain white rice thoroughly under cold running water until the water runs clear. For an extra creamy texture, you can soak the rice in water for 30 minutes before cooking, then drain well.

instructions.title: Cook the Congee Base
instructions.text: In a large pot, combine the rinsed rice with 8-10 cups of water or chicken broth. Bring it to a boil over high heat, then immediately reduce the heat to low. Cover and simmer gently for 30-40 minutes, stirring occasionally to prevent sticking, until the rice grains break down and the congee thickens to a creamy consistency. Add more hot water if the congee becomes too thick.

instructions.title: Prepare Century Eggs and Ginger
instructions.text: While the congee is simmering, peel the century eggs carefully. Dice them into small, bite-sized pieces. Peel and julienne or thinly slice the ginger. If using pork, thinly slice or shred it.

instructions.title: Add Century Eggs and Pork (Optional)
instructions.text: Once the congee has reached your desired consistency, stir in the diced century eggs and most of the julienned ginger. If using pork, add it now and cook for another 5-7 minutes until the pork is fully cooked through and tender. Stir gently to incorporate.

instructions.title: Season the Congee
instructions.text: Season the congee with salt and white pepper to taste. Start with a small amount and add more as needed. The century eggs provide a savory depth, so taste before adding too much salt.

instructions.title: Serve and Garnish
instructions.text: Ladle the hot congee into individual serving bowls. Garnish generously with chopped green onions and the remaining fresh julienned ginger. Drizzle with a touch of sesame oil if desired before serving immediately. Enjoy this comforting Chinese breakfast!

tips: For extra creamy congee, freeze the rinsed rice for at least 2 hours or overnight before cooking. The frozen grains crack more easily, speeding up the cooking time and resulting in a smoother texture.
When handling century eggs, use a sharp knife and clean it between cuts to prevent the sticky yolk from making a mess. You can also lightly oil your knife.
Congee thickens as it cools. If you have leftovers, add a splash of hot water or broth when reheating to achieve the desired consistency.
article:
Embrace the comforting embrace of a warm bowl of congee, a staple in Chinese cuisine that transcends mere sustenance to become a symbol of care and tradition. While congee comes in countless forms, one of the most iconic and beloved variations for breakfast is Congee with Century Egg. This classic dish, often enhanced with lean pork, offers a unique blend of textures and flavors that awaken the palate and provide a gentle start to the day. If you're looking to explore authentic Chinese breakfast recipes, diving into the world of congee, especially this particular iteration, is an absolute must.

## What is Congee with Century Egg?

Congee, also known as jook or rice porridge, is a savory rice gruel cooked until the grains break down and create a thick, creamy consistency. It’s a versatile dish, often served plain as a base for various toppings or infused with ingredients during the cooking process. Congee with Century Egg takes this comforting foundation and elevates it with the distinct flavor of pidan, or century egg.

A century egg is a preserved duck, chicken, or quail egg that has been cured in a mixture of clay, ash, salt, quicklime, and rice hulls for several weeks or months. Despite its somewhat intimidating appearance – a dark, translucent albumen and a creamy, greenish-grey yolk – its taste is surprisingly mild, earthy, and umami-rich, with a unique aroma that adds depth to the congee. When combined with tender slivers of lean pork and fragrant ginger, the century egg provides a delightful contrast in flavor and texture, making each spoonful of Congee with Century Egg a truly satisfying experience. It's not just food; it's a culinary hug, deeply ingrained in Chinese breakfast culture.

## Ingredients You Need

Crafting the perfect bowl of Congee with Century Egg requires a few key components. The beauty lies in the simplicity of these ingredients, allowing their individual flavors to shine through when combined.

*   1 cup long-grain jasmine rice (or short-grain rice for a creamier texture)
*   8-10 cups water or chicken broth (broth adds more flavor)
*   2-3 century eggs, peeled and diced
*   100-150g lean pork loin or tenderloin, thinly sliced or julienned
*   1-inch piece of ginger, peeled and thinly sliced or julienned
*   2 scallions, green parts only, chopped for garnish
*   1 teaspoon salt, or to taste
*   1/2 teaspoon white pepper, or to taste
*   1 tablespoon sesame oil (for drizzling)
*   Optional: 1 tablespoon Shaoxing wine (for marinating pork), 1 teaspoon cornstarch (for marinating pork)

## Step-by-Step Instructions

Preparing Congee with Century Egg is a patient process, but the delicious results are well worth the wait.

1.  **Prepare the Rice:** Rinse the rice thoroughly under cold running water until the water runs clear. For an extra creamy congee and reduced cooking time, you can soak the rice in water for at least 30 minutes, or even overnight. Some chefs also suggest freezing the soaked rice for a few hours, as the ice crystals help break down the rice grains faster when cooked.
2.  **Marinate the Pork (Optional but Recommended):** If using pork, combine the thinly sliced pork with a pinch of salt, a dash of white pepper, 1 tablespoon of Shaoxing wine, and 1 teaspoon of cornstarch. Mix well and set aside for at least 15 minutes. This tenderizes the pork and adds flavor.
3.  **Start the Congee Base:** In a large pot with a heavy bottom, combine the rinsed rice and water (or broth). Bring the mixture to a rolling boil over high heat, stirring occasionally to prevent the rice from sticking to the bottom.
4.  **Simmer and Stir:** Once boiling, reduce the heat to low, cover the pot partially (leaving a small gap to prevent boil-overs), and let it simmer gently for at least 1.5 to 2 hours. Stir every 15-20 minutes, especially towards the end, to ensure the rice doesn't stick and to achieve that signature creamy consistency. The rice grains should have completely broken down, and the liquid should be thick and milky. If the congee becomes too thick, you can add more hot water or broth.
5.  **Add Flavorings:** About 15-20 minutes before serving, add the thinly sliced ginger and the marinated pork (if using) to the congee. Stir gently to separate the pork slices and cook them through.
6.  **Incorporate Century Egg:** Once the pork is cooked, gently stir in the diced century eggs. Cook for another 5 minutes, allowing the flavors to meld, but be careful not to overcook the century eggs, as they can become rubbery.
7.  **Season and Serve:** Season the Congee with Century Egg with salt and white pepper to taste. Ladle the hot congee into individual bowls. Drizzle with a few drops of sesame oil and garnish generously with chopped scallions.

## Pro Tips for the Best Results

Achieving that perfect, silky-smooth Congee with Century Egg requires a few insider tricks:

*   **Rice Type Matters:** While jasmine rice is common, some prefer short-grain glutinous rice for an extra creamy texture. Experiment to find your preference.
*   **The Water-to-Rice Ratio:** A ratio of 8-10 parts liquid to 1 part rice is a good starting point. Adjust based on your desired thickness. Remember, you can always add more hot liquid if it gets too thick.
*   **Stir, Stir, Stir (But Not Too Much):** Initial stirring prevents sticking. Later, occasional stirring helps break down the rice and achieve creaminess. Excessive stirring can make it gluey.
*   **Broth for Depth:** Using chicken or pork broth instead of just water will significantly enhance the flavor profile of your congee, giving it a richer, more savory base.
*   **Don't Overcook Century Egg:** Add the century eggs towards the end of the cooking process. They are already cooked and only need to be warmed through. Overcooking can alter their texture and flavor negatively.
*   **Fresh Ginger is Key:** Fresh ginger adds a bright, pungent note that perfectly complements the richness of the century egg and pork. Don't skimp on it.

## Serving Suggestions

Congee with Century Egg is a meal in itself, but it truly shines when accompanied by a few traditional Chinese breakfast items.

*   **Youtiao (Chinese Fried Dough Sticks):** These crispy, airy dough sticks are perfect for dipping into the hot congee, soaking up all the delicious flavors.
*   **Additional Seasonings:** Have extra soy sauce, white pepper, and chili oil on the table for guests to customize their bowls to their liking. A dash of black vinegar can also add a delightful tang.
*   **Preserved Vegetables:** Small plates of pickled radish, fermented bean curd, or shredded preserved mustard greens offer a salty, crunchy contrast that cuts through the richness of the congee.
*   **Peanuts:** Roasted peanuts can add a lovely crunch and nutty flavor.

## Variations and Substitutions

The beauty of congee lies in its adaptability. Once you master the basic Congee with Century Egg, feel free to experiment:

*   **Other Meats:** Instead of pork, try shredded chicken breast, thinly sliced fish (like cod or snapper), or even ground beef. Add fish towards the very end, as it cooks quickly.
*   **Vegetarian Options:** Omit the pork and use vegetable broth. You can add mushrooms (shiitake, oyster), rehydrated dried tofu skin, or goji berries for an earthy sweetness. The century egg itself is a preserved egg, so it's not strictly vegan, but it can be part of a vegetarian diet.
*   **Salted Duck Egg:** For another layer of umami and a different texture, add diced salted duck egg alongside the century egg. The two complement each other beautifully.
*   **Different Garnishes:** Beyond scallions, consider a sprinkle of crispy fried shallots, cilantro, or a drizzle of chili oil for a kick.
*   **Nutritional Boost:** Stir in some finely chopped carrots or leafy greens like spinach or bok choy during the last few minutes of cooking for added vitamins and color.

Whether you're new to Chinese cuisine or a seasoned enthusiast, making your own Congee with Century Egg is a rewarding experience. It’s a dish that embodies comfort, tradition, and a delightful symphony of flavors, making it an ideal choice among Chinese breakfast recipes to share with family and friends. Enjoy the process, savor the aroma, and delight in every spoonful of this timeless classic.
